questions.text: I maintain an archive of past work so I can reference and build on it.
I routinely budget for things that enhance my personal quality of life.
I believe my constitutional calmness is an asset in uncertain situations.
I can deliver difficult news to someone without being significantly affected by their reaction.
I hold the view that the moral urgency of suffering is the same whether it belongs to someone I know or someone I will never meet.
I find that keeping my emotional state separate from my assessment of a situation is what allows me to be consistently helpful.
I create standard operating procedures for tasks I'll need to repeat.
I detect when someone uses humor to deflect from something they find painful.
I experience the feeling of being moved as a gentle warmth rather than a consuming fire.
I keep all my tasks in motion simultaneously, moving between them as energy allows.
I believe the most admirable people are those who give the most of themselves.
I prefer to experiment with a new tool or device hands-on rather than reading instructions.
I experience the sensation of calm not as something I achieve but as something I simply inhabit.
I hold the view that life without intense feeling would not feel like life at all.
I tend to view my personal bucket list as a binding contract with myself.
I routinely adjust my pace, my route, or my method to match what works best for others.
I tend to miss it when a person's agreement comes with a subtle qualifier that changes its meaning entirely.
I instinctively work to make sure information flows freely to everyone, not through me.
I often know what someone is about to say before they say it based on context cues.
I think that the discipline of maintaining emotional composure during human crises is a form of service that directly improves outcomes.
I do whatever task is in front of me without analyzing whether it's the optimal use of my time.
I often adapt to the norms and expectations already present in a group.
I often push for my preferred approach in group decisions.
After receiving bad news, I can feel the shock reverberating through my body for hours.
I am inclined to announce decisions first and explain my reasoning second.
I sense when someone is carefully managing how they are perceived.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering should be treated as cumulative — each encounter adds to the case for engagement, never subtracts from it.
I notice that my emotional sensitivity means I am deeply affected by the tone of a room, a text, or a glance.
When resources are scarce, I instinctively direct them toward other people first.
I hold the view that every instance of suffering I encounter that goes unaddressed represents a failure of my moral attention.
I often find myself telling others what needs to happen next.
I believe that taking the most interesting assignment for myself, rather than the most needed one, is fair.
I focus on what is being said rather than what is being implied.
I create templates and standard procedures so I don't have to reinvent the wheel.
I am the person who usually says, 'What does everyone think we should do?'
I tend to miss the way people calibrate how much personal information to share based on their read of the room.
